---
title: "Chebyshev's Inequality and the Concentration Idea"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20240519)

DENS   = "#2b3a67"     # densities / data
BOUND  = "#c1121f"     # the bound / tail region
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # exact / true values
GREY   = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This article opens the **tail-and-limit** machinery
> that closes Movement 4 of the Probability Foundations track. It is the concentration
> sibling of [PROB-20 *Moment Generating and Characteristic Functions*]: where PROB-20
> packages **all** of a distribution's moments into one transform, this article asks how
> much you can already say about a distribution's **tails** knowing only its first two
> moments — the mean and the variance. It assumes [PROB-5 *Expectation*] (the expectation
> operator, its linearity, and the law of the unconscious statistician), [PROB-6
> *Variance, Standard Deviation, and Variance as a Sum of Squares*] (the definition
> $\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[(X-\mu)^2]$ and the standard deviation), and [PROB-8
> *Independence and the i.i.d. Sample*] (the variance-of-a-sum rule under independence,
> which we need for the law-of-large-numbers sketch). Nothing above the Calc-1 floor is
> assumed; the one integral we lean on is a Calc-1 integral of a nonnegative function over
> a sub-region, used explicitly. It **sets up** [PROB-21 *Modes of Convergence*] — the
> notion of *convergence in probability* is precisely what Chebyshev delivers for the
> sample mean — and it hands the **weak law of large numbers** to [INFR-A2] (Statistical
> Inference), which *owns* the limit theorems and only borrows the inequality built here.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Suppose all you know about a random quantity is its mean and its variance — not its shape,
not its tails, nothing else. How much probability can possibly be sitting far from the
mean? This article answers that with two distribution-free bounds. **Markov's inequality**
says a nonnegative random variable rarely exceeds a large multiple of its average:
$P(X \ge a) \le E[X]/a$. **Chebyshev's inequality**, obtained from Markov in two lines,
says no distribution can place much probability many standard deviations from its mean:
$P(|X-\mu| \ge k\sigma) \le 1/k^2$. The single claim this article defends is that
**variance controls the tails — universally, with no distributional assumptions** — and
that this universality is exactly why averages of independent measurements become
predictable. We pay for the universality with looseness: against a friendly bell curve the
bound is wildly conservative, and we will see by how much. But against an *adversarial*
distribution the bound is tight — attained exactly — which is what makes it a theorem and
not a heuristic. We finish by turning the inequality on the sample mean to derive a clean
**weak law of large numbers** sketch: average enough independent measurements and the
average concentrates on the truth, with a rate the variance hands us for free.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
If $X$ is a **nonnegative** random variable, then for any threshold $a>0$,
$$
P(X \ge a) \;\le\; \frac{E[X]}{a}
\qquad\text{(\textbf{Markov}).}
$$
Apply this to the nonnegative variable $(X-\mu)^2$, where $\mu = E[X]$, and out drops
**Chebyshev**: for any $k>0$,
$$
P\big(|X-\mu| \ge k\sigma\big) \;\le\; \frac{1}{k^2},
\qquad \sigma = \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(X)}.
$$
These bounds use **only** the mean (Markov) or the mean and variance (Chebyshev) — *no*
knowledge of the distribution's shape. That is their power and their price: they hold for
*every* distribution with the stated moments, so they cannot be improved without knowing
more, and for the nice distributions you usually meet they are loose. The payoff is
**concentration**: applied to a sample mean $\bar X_n$, whose variance shrinks like $1/n$,
Chebyshev forces the probability of $\bar X_n$ landing far from the true mean to zero — a
distribution-free **law of large numbers**.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The two inequalities are almost trivial to prove — the
work of this article is not the algebra but the *understanding*: why "nonnegative" is the
hinge Markov turns on, why squaring the deviation is the move that converts Markov into
Chebyshev, exactly how loose the bound is and when it is instead razor-tight, and how a
bound about one random variable becomes a statement about the long-run reliability of
averages. Keep one question in view the whole way: **with only the mean and variance in
hand, how much probability can the distribution hide far from the center?**

# Why we are here: a guarantee without a distribution {#sec-why}

## The problem the inequality solves {#sec-why-problem}

Almost every probability calculation you have done so far started from a *known*
distribution. You were told $X$ is normal, or Poisson, or exponential, and from the full
density or mass function you could compute any tail probability you liked by integrating
or summing. That is a luxury. In practice you very often **do not know the distribution**.
You have measured a few summary numbers — an average and a spread — and someone asks a tail
question: *what is the chance this quantity is wildly off?*

Concretely: a factory line produces parts whose lengths have mean $\mu = 10$ mm and
standard deviation $\sigma = 0.1$ mm. You have not characterized the full distribution of
lengths — you only ran enough measurement to estimate the mean and the spread. A customer
asks: *what fraction of parts can possibly be off by more than $0.3$ mm?* If you knew the
lengths were normal you would answer "about $0.27\%$" from the bell curve. But you do
**not** know they are normal — the process could be skewed, bimodal, contaminated by a rare
miscalibration. Can you say *anything* guaranteed, using only the two numbers you have?

That is the question Chebyshev answers, and the answer is yes: **no more than $1/3^2 \approx
11\%$ of parts can be off by more than $3\sigma = 0.3$ mm**, whatever the true distribution
is. It is a far weaker statement than the normal's $0.27\%$ — but it is *unconditional*. You
did not have to assume a shape you could not justify.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If the normal-curve answer is so much sharper, why would anyone use the loose Chebyshev
number?"* Because the sharp answer is only valid **if the normal assumption is true**, and
in the situation Chebyshev is built for you have no license to assume it. A bound that
holds for *every* distribution with the given mean and variance cannot be beaten by an
adversary who picks the worst distribution consistent with your two numbers — and "the
adversary picks the worst case" is exactly the contract a *guarantee* must honor.
Chebyshev trades sharpness for safety; you reach for it precisely when you cannot afford
to be wrong about the shape.
:::

## What came before, and the historical thread {#sec-why-history}

The intuition that "spread limits how far things stray" is old, but the clean inequality
has a clear lineage. The French mathematician **Irénée-Jules Bienaymé** stated the
variance-based bound in 1853, in work on the method of least squares. The Russian
mathematician **Pafnuty Chebyshev** — and his student **Andrey Markov** — gave it the
form and the role it has today, around 1867, as the engine of Chebyshev's proof of the
**law of large numbers**. (For this reason the variance bound is sometimes called the
Bienaymé–Chebyshev inequality, and the underlying one-sided bound on a nonnegative
variable carries Markov's name.) The motivation was exactly the *why* above: Chebyshev
wanted to prove that sample averages converge to expectations **without assuming the
measurements were normal** — Laplace and Gauss had leaned heavily on the normal
approximation, and Chebyshev's school wanted a rigorous result that did not. The
inequality is the tool that made a *distribution-free* law of large numbers possible, and
that is the thread we hold for the rest of the article: the inequality exists to buy a
guarantee when you refuse to assume the shape.

This is why the inequality is built the way it is. It uses the variance — the one spread
summary that is *additive* across independent variables (from [PROB-8]) — because the goal
was always to control the average of *many* independent things, where additivity makes the
spread of the average collapse. Markov-then-Chebyshev is not an arbitrary chain; it is the
shortest path from "a nonnegative thing rarely gets huge" to "the average rarely strays,"
which is the destination [INFR-A2] builds toward.

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, here is the whole idea in one picture. Variance is the average squared
distance from the mean: $\operatorname{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2]$. It is a fixed,
**finite budget** of squared deviation, paid out across all the outcomes according to how
likely they are. Now ask: how much probability can sit *far* from the mean, say beyond
$k$ standard deviations?

Every outcome out there contributes at least $(k\sigma)^2 = k^2\sigma^2$ to that budget —
its squared distance is at least that large. So if a probability mass $p$ sits beyond
$k\sigma$, it alone consumes at least $p \cdot k^2\sigma^2$ of the budget. But the *total*
budget is only $\sigma^2$. Therefore
$$
p \cdot k^2 \sigma^2 \;\le\; \sigma^2
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p \;\le\; \frac{1}{k^2}.
$$
That is Chebyshev, and it is nothing more than "you cannot spend more squared deviation
than you have." Far-out mass is *expensive* — each unit of it costs $k^2\sigma^2$ — so a
finite variance can only afford a little of it. Squaring is what makes distance expensive:
it punishes far outcomes quadratically, so a small variance forcibly starves the tails.

```{python}
#| label: fig-budget
#| fig-cap: "Variance as a finite budget that starves the tails. The blue density has mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$. The shaded red regions are the tails beyond $k=2$ standard deviations. Every outcome in those tails contributes at least $(2\\sigma)^2 = 4$ to the variance budget, yet the *whole* budget is only $\\sigma^2 = 1$. So at most a fraction $1/k^2 = 1/4$ of probability can live out there — Chebyshev's ceiling (red dashed). The actual tail mass for this particular density is far below the ceiling, which previews how loose the universal bound is for friendly shapes."
mu, sigma, k = 0.0, 1.0, 2.0
x = np.linspace(-5, 5, 1000)
# A concrete friendly density (standard normal) to visualize against the budget.
f = np.exp(-x**2 / 2) / np.sqrt(2 * np.pi)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, f, color=DENS, label="a density with $\\mu=0,\\ \\sigma=1$")
tail = np.abs(x - mu) >= k * sigma
ax.fill_between(x, 0, f, where=tail, color=BOUND, alpha=0.35,
                label=f"tails: $|x-\\mu|\\ge {k:.0f}\\sigma$")
ax.axvline(mu + k * sigma, color=BOUND, lw=1, ls=":")
ax.axvline(mu - k * sigma, color=BOUND, lw=1, ls=":")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Far-out mass is expensive: at most $1/k^2$ can afford to be there")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

# Markov's inequality {#sec-markov}

Everything starts from a one-line bound for **nonnegative** random variables. The
nonnegativity is not decoration — it is the entire engine, as the proof will make
unmistakable.

::: {#thm-markov}
**Theorem (Markov's inequality).** Let $X$ be a random variable with $X \ge 0$ (it never
takes a negative value), and suppose its mean $E[X]$ is finite. Then for every real number
$a > 0$,
$$
P(X \ge a) \;\le\; \frac{E[X]}{a}.
$$ {#eq-markov}
:::

::: {.proof}
We give the argument in full, defining each object as it appears. The key device is an
**indicator random variable**. For the event $\{X \ge a\}$, define
$$
\mathbf 1\{X \ge a\} \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if the outcome has } X \ge a,\\
0, & \text{if the outcome has } X < a.
\end{cases}
$$
This is itself a random variable — a function of the outcome — taking only the values $0$
and $1$. Its expectation is exactly the probability of the event, because for a
$\{0,1\}$-valued variable the average *is* the probability of being $1$:
$$
E\big[\mathbf 1\{X \ge a\}\big]
= 1\cdot P(X \ge a) + 0 \cdot P(X < a)
= P(X \ge a).
$$ {#eq-indicator}
That identity — expectation of an indicator equals probability of its event — is the
bridge we will cross.

Now the one inequality that does all the work. We claim that, **for every outcome**,
$$
a \cdot \mathbf 1\{X \ge a\} \;\le\; X.
$$ {#eq-pointwise}
Check it outcome by outcome, the only two cases there are:

- If the outcome has $X \ge a$, the indicator is $1$, so the left side is $a$, and the
  right side is $X \ge a$. So $a \le X$ holds. ✓
- If the outcome has $X < a$, the indicator is $0$, so the left side is $0$, and the right
  side is $X$, which is $\ge 0$ **because $X$ is nonnegative**. So $0 \le X$ holds. ✓

This second case is where nonnegativity is spent: without $X \ge 0$, the right side could
be negative and the inequality $0 \le X$ would fail. That is why Markov is a theorem about
nonnegative variables and not about all of them.

Since @eq-pointwise holds for every outcome, it holds in expectation — expectation
preserves inequalities, because averaging a quantity that is always $\le$ another averages
to something $\le$ (this **monotonicity of expectation** is established in [PROB-5]; it
follows because $X - a\,\mathbf 1\{X\ge a\} \ge 0$ pointwise and the expectation of a
nonnegative variable is nonnegative). Taking $E[\cdot]$ of both sides of @eq-pointwise and
using linearity to pull the constant $a$ out of the left side:
$$
a \cdot E\big[\mathbf 1\{X \ge a\}\big] \;\le\; E[X].
$$
Substitute @eq-indicator on the left, $E[\mathbf 1\{X\ge a\}] = P(X\ge a)$:
$$
a \cdot P(X \ge a) \;\le\; E[X].
$$
Divide both sides by $a$, which is allowed and preserves the direction because $a>0$:
$$
P(X \ge a) \;\le\; \frac{E[X]}{a}. \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

A second way to see the same thing, for those who prefer the integral picture, is to write
the mean of a nonnegative continuous $X$ with density $f$ and *throw away* the part of the
integral below $a$:
$$
E[X] = \int_0^\infty x\, f(x)\,dx
\;\ge\; \int_a^\infty x\, f(x)\,dx
\;\ge\; \int_a^\infty a\, f(x)\,dx
= a\int_a^\infty f(x)\,dx
= a\,P(X\ge a).
$$
The first $\ge$ drops the nonnegative chunk $\int_0^a x f\,dx$ (legal because the
integrand $xf \ge 0$ there — again nonnegativity); the second $\ge$ replaces $x$ by the
smaller $a$ throughout the region $x \ge a$. Rearranging gives @eq-markov. Both routes
spend the same coin: a nonnegative variable cannot hide a lot of mass at large values
without inflating its own average.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What's the catch with Markov — when is it useless?"* Two ways. First, it needs $X \ge
0$; for a variable that can go negative it says nothing directly (you apply it to $|X|$ or
$X^2$ instead — which is exactly the trick that yields Chebyshev). Second, it only bounds
the **upper** tail and only for $a > E[X]$: if $a \le E[X]$ the bound $E[X]/a \ge 1$ is
vacuous, since probabilities never exceed $1$ anyway. Markov is sharpest when $a$ is a
large multiple of the mean.
:::

# Chebyshev's inequality {#sec-chebyshev}

Now the headline result. The move is to apply Markov not to $X$ but to the **squared
deviation** $(X-\mu)^2$, which is automatically nonnegative — manufacturing the
nonnegative variable Markov demands out of any variable at all.

::: {#thm-chebyshev}
**Theorem (Chebyshev's inequality).** Let $X$ have finite mean $\mu = E[X]$ and finite
variance $\sigma^2 = \operatorname{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2]$, with $\sigma > 0$. Then for
every $\varepsilon > 0$,
$$
P\big(|X - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\big) \;\le\; \frac{\sigma^2}{\varepsilon^2}.
$$ {#eq-chebyshev-eps}
Writing the threshold in units of the standard deviation, $\varepsilon = k\sigma$ with
$k>0$, this is the familiar
$$
P\big(|X-\mu| \ge k\sigma\big) \;\le\; \frac{1}{k^2}.
$$ {#eq-chebyshev-k}
:::

::: {.proof}
Define the new random variable $Y = (X-\mu)^2$. It is nonnegative for every outcome,
because it is a square of a real number — so Markov applies to it. Its mean is, by the
definition of variance from [PROB-6],
$$
E[Y] = E\big[(X-\mu)^2\big] = \operatorname{Var}(X) = \sigma^2.
$$
Next, translate the event we care about into an event about $Y$. The two events are
**identical**, outcome for outcome:
$$
|X - \mu| \ge \varepsilon
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
(X-\mu)^2 \ge \varepsilon^2
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
Y \ge \varepsilon^2 .
$$
The first equivalence is just squaring a nonnegative inequality: for nonnegative numbers,
$u \ge v$ holds exactly when $u^2 \ge v^2$ (the function $u\mapsto u^2$ is strictly
increasing on $[0,\infty)$). Both $|X-\mu|$ and $\varepsilon$ are nonnegative, so squaring
both sides is reversible — no information is lost, the events are the same set of outcomes.
Therefore their probabilities are equal:
$$
P\big(|X-\mu| \ge \varepsilon\big) = P\big(Y \ge \varepsilon^2\big).
$$
Now apply Markov's inequality @eq-markov to the nonnegative variable $Y$ with the
threshold $a = \varepsilon^2 > 0$:
$$
P\big(Y \ge \varepsilon^2\big) \;\le\; \frac{E[Y]}{\varepsilon^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{\varepsilon^2}.
$$
Chaining the equality and the bound gives @eq-chebyshev-eps. Finally substitute
$\varepsilon = k\sigma$: the right side becomes $\sigma^2/(k\sigma)^2 =
\sigma^2/(k^2\sigma^2) = 1/k^2$, which is @eq-chebyshev-k. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So Chebyshev is *Markov in a costume*: the only idea is that squaring turns any deviation
into a nonnegative quantity whose mean is the variance, after which Markov does the rest.
Read @eq-chebyshev-k aloud: **the probability of landing $k$ or more standard deviations
from the mean is at most $1/k^2$, for any distribution whatsoever.** Two standard
deviations out: at most $1/4 = 25\%$. Three out: at most $1/9 \approx 11\%$. Ten out: at
most $1\%$. These are ceilings nobody — no distribution with that mean and variance — can
exceed.

Note the bound is **vacuous for $k \le 1$**: at one standard deviation, $1/k^2 = 1$, and
"the probability is at most $1$" says nothing. Chebyshev only bites once you ask about
deviations strictly larger than one standard deviation, which is exactly the regime "far
from the mean" you care about.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why square the deviation — why not use $|X-\mu|$ directly with Markov?"* You can, and it
gives a *different*, sometimes worse, bound. Applying Markov to $|X-\mu|$ needs $E|X-\mu|$,
the **mean absolute deviation**, which is a moment most problems do not hand you and which
is not additive over independent sums. Squaring buys two things at once: it produces a
quantity whose mean is the **variance** — the spread summary you usually *do* have and that
*is* additive (from [PROB-8]) — and it makes far deviations cost quadratically, giving the
characteristic $1/k^2$ decay instead of $1/k$. The quadratic penalty is also why Chebyshev
plugs straight into the variance-shrinks-like-$1/n$ argument for the law of large numbers.
:::

# How loose is it? The honest accounting {#sec-looseness}

A guarantee that holds for *every* distribution must brace for the worst one, so for any
*particular* nice distribution it will overshoot. Let us measure the overshoot honestly,
because knowing the size of the gap is what tells you when Chebyshev is a useful working
number and when it is almost comically conservative.

Take the standard normal $Z \sim \mathcal N(0,1)$, where $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$.
Chebyshev says $P(|Z| \ge k) \le 1/k^2$. The *true* tail probability, from the normal CDF,
is much smaller. The table below puts the ceiling beside the truth.

```{python}
#| label: fig-looseness
#| fig-cap: "How loose Chebyshev is for a friendly distribution. For the standard normal, the red curve is Chebyshev's universal ceiling $1/k^2$ on $P(|Z|\\ge k)$; the blue curve is the *actual* normal tail probability $2(1-\\Phi(k))$. On a log scale the gap is enormous — at $k=4$ the bound permits $6.25\\%$ while the true tail is $0.006\\%$, a factor of about a thousand. The looseness is the price of distribution-freeness: the bound also has to cover distributions whose tails really are this heavy."
from math import erf, sqrt

def normal_two_sided_tail(k):
    # P(|Z| >= k) = 2 * (1 - Phi(k)); Phi via the error function.
    Phi = 0.5 * (1 + erf(k / sqrt(2)))
    return 2 * (1 - Phi)

ks = np.linspace(1.0, 5.0, 400)
cheb = 1.0 / ks**2
true_tail = np.array([normal_two_sided_tail(k) for k in ks])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(ks, cheb, color=BOUND, label="Chebyshev ceiling $1/k^2$")
ax.plot(ks, true_tail, color=DENS, label="true normal tail $P(|Z|\\ge k)$")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("$k$ (standard deviations from the mean)")
ax.set_ylabel("tail probability (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Universal bound vs. the actual normal tail")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

So for a Gaussian the bound is loose by **orders of magnitude**, and it gets looser the
further out you go. Does that make Chebyshev useless? No — it makes it *honest*. The bound
cannot be sharper, because there genuinely exist distributions with mean $0$ and variance
$1$ whose tail at $k$ is as heavy as $1/k^2$ allows. The next section exhibits one and
shows the bound is **attained**.

## The bound is tight: an adversarial distribution {#sec-tightness}

A bound is only worth trusting if it cannot be improved without extra assumptions. We show
Chebyshev is **tight** by constructing, for any chosen $k>1$, a distribution that *hits*
the bound exactly: $P(|X-\mu| \ge k\sigma) = 1/k^2$.

::: {#thm-tight}
**Theorem (tightness of Chebyshev).** For every $k > 1$ there is a random variable $X$
with finite mean and variance for which @eq-chebyshev-k holds with **equality**.
:::

::: {.proof}
Let $X$ be the discrete variable that places its mass at three points: $-c$, $0$, and
$+c$, where $c>0$ is a number we will choose, with probabilities
$$
P(X = -c) = \frac{1}{2k^2}, \qquad
P(X = 0) = 1 - \frac{1}{k^2}, \qquad
P(X = +c) = \frac{1}{2k^2}.
$$
These are valid probabilities: each is in $[0,1]$ for $k>1$ (so $1/k^2 < 1$), and they sum
to $\tfrac{1}{2k^2} + (1 - \tfrac1{k^2}) + \tfrac1{2k^2} = 1$. Computing the mean directly
from the definition $E[X] = \sum_x x\,P(X=x)$, the equal-and-opposite outer masses cancel
term for term:
$$
\mu = E[X] = (-c)\tfrac1{2k^2} + 0\cdot(1-\tfrac1{k^2}) + (c)\tfrac1{2k^2}
= -\tfrac{c}{2k^2} + \tfrac{c}{2k^2} = 0.
$$
The variance is $E[(X-\mu)^2] = E[X^2]$ since $\mu = 0$:
$$
\sigma^2 = E[X^2] = c^2\cdot\tfrac1{2k^2} + 0 + c^2\cdot\tfrac1{2k^2} = \frac{c^2}{k^2}.
$$
So $\sigma = c/k$, i.e. $c = k\sigma$. The only outcomes that land at distance $\ge
k\sigma = c$ from the mean are $X = \pm c$, which together carry probability
$\tfrac1{2k^2} + \tfrac1{2k^2} = \tfrac1{k^2}$. Therefore
$$
P\big(|X - \mu| \ge k\sigma\big) = P(X = -c) + P(X = c) = \frac{1}{k^2},
$$
exactly the Chebyshev ceiling. Equality holds. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the whole moral of the inequality. The worst case is a distribution that puts a
sliver of mass *exactly* at $k$ standard deviations and dumps the rest at the mean — a
"barbell." Real distributions spread their tail mass out *past* $k\sigma$ and so pay more
variance per unit of tail probability, which is why they fall short of the ceiling. Against
the barbell, Chebyshev is not loose at all; it is exact. A theorem must survive its worst
adversary, and the barbell is it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-tight
#| fig-cap: "The adversary that makes Chebyshev exact. For $k=2$, the three-point 'barbell' distribution puts mass $1/(2k^2)=1/8$ at each of $\\pm 2\\sigma$ and the remaining $1-1/k^2 = 3/4$ at the mean. Exactly $1/k^2 = 1/4$ of the probability sits at distance $\\ge 2\\sigma$ (red), so Chebyshev's bound (red dashed line at $0.25$) is attained with equality. No distribution with this mean and variance can do worse, which is what makes $1/k^2$ a genuine ceiling and not just a heuristic."
k = 2.0
sigma = 1.0
c = k * sigma
locs = np.array([-c, 0.0, c])
probs = np.array([1/(2*k**2), 1 - 1/k**2, 1/(2*k**2)])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
colors = [BOUND, DENS, BOUND]
ax.bar(locs, probs, width=0.18, color=colors, edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.6)
for x, p in zip(locs, probs):
    ax.text(x, p + 0.02, f"{p:.3f}", ha="center", fontsize=9)
ax.axhline(1/k**2, color=BOUND, ls=":", lw=1.2,
           label="Chebyshev ceiling $1/k^2 = 0.25$ on the tail mass")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$")
ax.set_ylabel("probability mass")
ax.set_title("The three-point barbell attains Chebyshev exactly ($k=2$)")
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.85)
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

# From one variable to the average: the concentration payoff {#sec-concentration}

Now the reason Chebyshev earns its place at the root of the series. The inequality is mild
when applied to a single variable, but it becomes powerful the moment we apply it to a
**sample mean**, because averaging *crushes the variance*, and Chebyshev converts that
crushed variance directly into a vanishing tail probability.

## The variance of the sample mean {#sec-var-mean}

Let $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ be **independent and identically distributed** (i.i.d.) — the
setting built in [PROB-8] — each with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$. Their **sample
mean** is the random variable
$$
\bar X_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i .
$$
We need its mean and variance. By linearity of expectation (from [PROB-5], which holds with
or without independence),
$$
E[\bar X_n] = \frac1n \sum_{i=1}^n E[X_i] = \frac1n \cdot n\mu = \mu .
$$
So the sample mean is **centered on the truth** — its expectation is the very $\mu$ we are
trying to pin down. For the variance we use the rule from [PROB-8] that the variance of a
sum of **independent** variables is the sum of the variances, together with the scaling
rule $\operatorname{Var}(aY) = a^2\operatorname{Var}(Y)$ from [PROB-6]:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)
= \operatorname{Var}\!\left(\frac1n \sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\cdot n\sigma^2
= \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.
$$ {#eq-var-mean}
There it is — the fact that makes everything work: **averaging $n$ independent measurements
divides the variance by $n$.** The spread of the average shrinks; its standard deviation
falls like $\sigma/\sqrt n$. Independence is essential here: it is what lets the variance of
the sum be the sum of the variances, with no covariance cross-terms surviving (the general
$\operatorname{Var}(\sum X_i)$ with dependence carries $2\sum_{i<j}\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j)$
terms, from [PROB-7], which need not vanish).

## The weak law of large numbers, from Chebyshev {#sec-wlln}

Apply Chebyshev @eq-chebyshev-eps to $\bar X_n$, whose mean is $\mu$ and whose variance is
$\sigma^2/n$:

::: {#thm-wlln}
**Theorem (weak law of large numbers, Chebyshev's version).** Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be
i.i.d. with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$. Then for every fixed
$\varepsilon > 0$,
$$
P\big(|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\big) \;\le\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n\,\varepsilon^2}
\;\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\; 0 .
$$ {#eq-wlln}
That is, the sample mean **converges in probability** to $\mu$: no matter how small a
tolerance $\varepsilon$ you demand, the chance the average misses the true mean by more
than $\varepsilon$ can be driven to zero by taking enough samples.
:::

::: {.proof}
Chebyshev @eq-chebyshev-eps applied to the random variable $\bar X_n$ — using
$E[\bar X_n] = \mu$ from above and $\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2/n$ from
@eq-var-mean — gives directly
$$
P\big(|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\big)
\;\le\; \frac{\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)}{\varepsilon^2}
= \frac{\sigma^2/n}{\varepsilon^2}
= \frac{\sigma^2}{n\,\varepsilon^2}.
$$
Now $\varepsilon$ is held fixed and $\sigma^2$ is a fixed finite constant, so the right
side is a constant divided by $n$; as $n\to\infty$ it tends to $0$. Since a probability is
nonnegative and bounded above by something going to $0$, it goes to $0$ as well. That is
exactly the definition of $\bar X_n \to \mu$ **in probability** (the mode of convergence
made precise in [PROB-21]). $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the destination the *why* (@sec-why-history) was driving at. Chebyshev wanted a law
of large numbers that **did not assume the measurements were normal** — and here it is, for
*any* distribution with a finite variance, falling straight out of the inequality in three
lines. The bound even hands you a **rate** and therefore a **sample-size formula**: to be
within $\varepsilon$ of $\mu$ with confidence at least $1-\delta$, set the right side equal
to $\delta$ and solve, $n \ge \sigma^2/(\delta\varepsilon^2)$. Distribution-free, no
Gaussian assumed.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lln
#| fig-cap: "Chebyshev driving the law of large numbers. We sample i.i.d. from a heavily skewed Exponential($\\lambda=1$) population ($\\mu=1$, $\\sigma=1$ — emphatically *not* normal) and track the sample mean as $n$ grows, for ten independent runs (thin grey paths). The fan of paths narrows toward the true mean $\\mu=1$ (green). The red envelope is Chebyshev's $\\pm\\,\\varepsilon$ band where $\\varepsilon$ is chosen so the bound $\\sigma^2/(n\\varepsilon^2)$ equals $0.05$ at each $n$ — i.e. the guaranteed-95\\% region. Every path stays inside its envelope far more often than the worst case allows, because the exponential is friendlier than the barbell adversary."
n_max = 2000
n_runs = 10
mu_true, sigma_true = 1.0, 1.0  # Exponential(1)

ns = np.arange(1, n_max + 1)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for _ in range(n_runs):
    draws = rng.exponential(scale=1.0, size=n_max)
    running_mean = np.cumsum(draws) / ns
    ax.plot(ns, running_mean, color=GREY, alpha=0.6, lw=1.0)

# Chebyshev 95% envelope: sigma^2/(n eps^2) = 0.05  =>  eps = sigma*sqrt(1/(0.05 n)).
delta = 0.05
eps_band = sigma_true * np.sqrt(1.0 / (delta * ns))
ax.plot(ns, mu_true + eps_band, color=BOUND, lw=1.4, ls="--",
        label="Chebyshev 95% band $\\mu\\pm\\varepsilon$")
ax.plot(ns, mu_true - eps_band, color=BOUND, lw=1.4, ls="--")
ax.axhline(mu_true, color=ACCENT, lw=1.6, label="true mean $\\mu=1$")
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_ylim(0.0, 2.0)
ax.set_xlabel("$n$ (sample size, log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("$\\bar X_n$")
ax.set_title("The sample mean concentrates — guaranteed, with no normal assumption")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Chebyshev gives a $1/n$ rate for the tail probability — but the central limit theorem
says the sample mean spreads like $1/\sqrt n$. Which is right?"* Both, and they answer
different questions. The CLT (owned by [INFR-A2]) describes the *typical* fluctuation:
$\bar X_n - \mu$ is of order $\sigma/\sqrt n$, the standard deviation from @eq-var-mean.
Chebyshev bounds the *probability* of an atypical fluctuation: for a **fixed** $\varepsilon$
that does not shrink with $n$, that probability decays at least like $1/n$. There is no
contradiction — one is about the scale of normal-sized wiggles, the other about the chance
of a large, fixed-size miss. Chebyshev is weaker than the CLT (it gives a polynomial $1/n$
where sharper tools give exponential decay), but it is also far more robust: it needs only
a finite variance, not the i.i.d.-plus-finite-third-moment machinery the CLT's rate
refinements lean on.
:::

# Worked examples, including the edge of applicability {#sec-examples}

We carry three honest examples end to end: one where Chebyshev is exactly the right tool,
one where it is usable but loose, and one at the **edge of applicability** where it breaks
because its single assumption — a finite variance — fails.

## Example 1 — A distribution-free quality guarantee (the right tool) {#sec-ex-quality}

Return to the factory from @sec-why-problem. Part lengths have mean $\mu = 10$ mm and
standard deviation $\sigma = 0.1$ mm; the distribution's *shape* is unknown and possibly
skewed by occasional miscalibration. A part is "out of spec" if it is off by more than
$0.3$ mm. We want a guaranteed cap on the out-of-spec fraction.

Here $0.3$ mm $= 3\sigma$, so $k = 3$, and Chebyshev gives
$$
P(|X - 10| \ge 0.3) = P(|X-\mu| \ge 3\sigma) \le \frac{1}{3^2} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.111 .
$$
**At most $11.1\%$** of parts can be out of spec — a contractual guarantee that survives
any distribution consistent with the two measured numbers. This is exactly the situation
Chebyshev was built for: you have moments but not a shape, and you need a bound nobody can
break. If you later *earn* the right to assume near-normality (by actually characterizing
the process), you would replace $11.1\%$ with the far smaller $0.27\%$ — but only then.

## Example 2 — Polling sample size, distribution-free (usable but loose) {#sec-ex-poll}

You want to estimate the fraction $p$ of voters favoring a measure by sampling $n$ people
i.i.d. and reporting the sample proportion $\hat p = \bar X_n$, where each $X_i \in
\{0,1\}$ is a Bernoulli($p$) response. How large must $n$ be so the estimate is within
$\varepsilon = 0.03$ of the truth with probability at least $95\%$, *without* invoking the
normal approximation?

Each $X_i$ has variance $\sigma^2 = p(1-p)$, which we do not know — but it is **at most
$1/4$**, attained at $p = 1/2$ (a one-line calculus check: $p(1-p)$ is a downward parabola
maximized at $p=1/2$, value $1/4$). Using the worst case $\sigma^2 = 1/4$, the WLLN bound
@eq-wlln demands
$$
\frac{\sigma^2}{n\varepsilon^2} \le 0.05
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
n \ge \frac{\sigma^2}{0.05\,\varepsilon^2}
= \frac{1/4}{0.05 \cdot (0.03)^2}
= \frac{0.25}{0.05 \cdot 0.0009}
= \frac{0.25}{0.000045}
\approx 5556 .
$$
So **$n \approx 5{,}556$** guarantees the bound. This is *correct and distribution-free*,
but notice it is roughly five times larger than the $n \approx 1{,}068$ the normal
approximation (CLT) would prescribe for the same target. The looseness from @sec-looseness
shows up as concrete extra cost: you buy a guarantee that holds against the barbell
adversary, and you pay for it in sample size. The honest reading: use Chebyshev to get a
*safe* sample size when you cannot justify the CLT, and tighten with the CLT once $n$ is
large enough for the normal approximation to be trustworthy.

```{python}
#| label: fig-poll
#| fig-cap: "Chebyshev vs. the normal approximation for polling sample size. Both give the $n$ needed so the sample proportion is within $\\varepsilon$ of $p$ with 95% confidence, worst case $p=1/2$. The red Chebyshev curve ($n = \\sigma^2/(0.05\\varepsilon^2)$) sits well above the blue normal-approximation curve ($n = (z_{0.975}\\,\\sigma/\\varepsilon)^2$) at every margin of error: the distribution-free guarantee costs about $5\\times$ the samples. The vertical line marks the $\\varepsilon=0.03$ worked example."
eps_grid = np.linspace(0.01, 0.06, 200)
sig2 = 0.25  # worst-case Bernoulli variance
z = 1.959963985  # 97.5th percentile of the standard normal
n_cheb = sig2 / (0.05 * eps_grid**2)
n_clt = (z**2 * sig2) / (eps_grid**2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(eps_grid, n_cheb, color=BOUND, label="Chebyshev (distribution-free)")
ax.plot(eps_grid, n_clt, color=DENS, label="normal approximation (CLT)")
ax.axvline(0.03, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1)
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("margin of error $\\varepsilon$")
ax.set_ylabel("required sample size $n$ (log scale)")
ax.set_title("The price of a distribution-free guarantee")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Example 3 — The edge of applicability: a heavy tail with no variance {#sec-ex-cauchy}

Chebyshev's one assumption is a **finite variance**. When that fails, the inequality says
*nothing* — and worse, the conclusion it would have given is *false*. This is the honest
boundary, and it is not exotic: heavy-tailed data (financial returns, file sizes, insurance
losses, network traffic) routinely strain or break the finite-variance assumption.

The cleanest break is the **standard Cauchy distribution**, with density
$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\pi(1 + x^2)}, \qquad -\infty < x < \infty .
$$
Its tails decay only like $1/x^2$. Already its **mean does not exist** (the defining
integral $\int x f(x)\,dx$ fails to converge), let alone its variance — so neither Markov
on $|X|$ nor Chebyshev can even be *stated*: there is no $\mu$ and no $\sigma$ to plug in.
The consequence is dramatic and worth seeing: the **sample mean of i.i.d. Cauchy variables
does not concentrate at all.** In fact $\bar X_n$ has the *same* standard Cauchy
distribution as a single observation, for every $n$ — averaging buys you nothing. The law
of large numbers, which we derived from Chebyshev, **simply fails** here, because the
variance that drove @eq-var-mean to $0$ does not exist.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cauchy
#| fig-cap: "Where Chebyshev — and the law of large numbers — break. The blue paths are running sample means of i.i.d. Exponential(1) data (finite variance): they converge to the true mean, exactly as @thm-wlln guarantees. The red paths are running sample means of i.i.d. standard Cauchy data (no finite mean or variance): they never settle — fresh extreme observations jolt the average no matter how large $n$ is. Chebyshev cannot be applied to the Cauchy, and the failure is real, not a technicality: the sample mean does not concentrate. When your data looks like the red paths, reach for medians and robust estimators, not the mean."
n_max = 3000
ns = np.arange(1, n_max + 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for _ in range(4):
    exp_draws = rng.exponential(scale=1.0, size=n_max)
    ax.plot(ns, np.cumsum(exp_draws) / ns, color=DENS, alpha=0.7, lw=1.0)
for _ in range(4):
    cauchy_draws = rng.standard_cauchy(size=n_max)
    ax.plot(ns, np.cumsum(cauchy_draws) / ns, color=BOUND, alpha=0.7, lw=1.0)

ax.axhline(1.0, color=ACCENT, lw=1.4, ls="--", label="Exponential true mean $=1$")
ax.set_ylim(-6, 8)
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("$n$ (sample size, log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("running sample mean $\\bar X_n$")
ax.set_title("Finite variance (blue) concentrates; no variance (red) does not")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The lesson generalizes past the Cauchy. Any distribution with **infinite variance** — a
power-law tail with exponent $\le 2$ — escapes Chebyshev entirely, and the sample mean is a
poor, unstable summary. The practitioner's move when you suspect this regime is to (a)
check the tails (a log-log plot of the empirical survival function, the median-vs-mean
gap), and (b) switch to estimators that *do* have a guarantee under heavy tails — the
**median** and other quantiles (which exist for any distribution), or **robust /
trimmed** means — rather than forcing the mean and a variance-based bound onto data that
has neither.

# When to reach for Chebyshev — and when not {#sec-applicability}

Pulling the judgment together explicitly, as the goal demands.

**Reach for Markov / Chebyshev when:**

- You have a **mean and a variance but not a distributional shape**, and you need a tail
  bound you can defend against any shape — quality guarantees, worst-case sample sizes,
  service-level promises. This is the home turf (@sec-ex-quality).
- You need a **distribution-free** result on principle — a proof or guarantee that must not
  smuggle in normality. This is why Chebyshev sits under the weak law of large numbers and
  under [PROB-21]'s convergence-in-probability statements.
- You want a quick, conservative **sanity ceiling** on a tail before investing in a sharper
  analysis (@sec-ex-poll).

**Do not reach for it — use something else — when:**

- You **know (or can justify) the distribution.** If normality is warranted, the normal
  tail or the CLT gives an answer orders of magnitude sharper (@sec-looseness). Chebyshev's
  $11\%$ versus the normal's $0.27\%$ is the gap you are leaving on the table.
- You need the **sharpest possible tail** and you can afford an assumption. For variables
  with a moment generating function ([PROB-20]), **Chernoff bounds** apply Markov to
  $e^{tX}$ and deliver *exponential* tail decay $e^{-c k^2}$ instead of the polynomial
  $1/k^2$ — vastly tighter, at the cost of needing the MGF to exist.
- The **variance does not exist** (heavy tails, @sec-ex-cauchy). Chebyshev cannot be
  applied and the mean itself is the wrong summary; switch to medians, quantiles, or robust
  estimators.
- Your data is **strongly dependent.** The WLLN derivation leaned on independence to get
  $\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2/n$ (@sec-var-mean); under heavy dependence the
  covariance cross-terms ([PROB-7]) keep the variance of the average from shrinking, and
  the concentration argument stalls.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Chebyshev is a one-way bound, never an estimate of the tail.** It tells you the tail is
*no larger* than $1/k^2$; it never tells you the tail *is* that large. Reporting "about
$11\%$ of parts are out of spec" from @sec-ex-quality would be wrong — the true fraction
could be a tiny fraction of a percent. Use it as a ceiling, not a point estimate. The
second classic error is **applying it to a variable with no finite variance** (or to a
non-nonnegative variable in Markov's case) and trusting the output: there is no output, the
hypothesis failed, and the conclusion can be flatly false (@sec-ex-cauchy).
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You have the first piece of Movement 4's tail-and-limit machinery: a distribution-free grip
on how much probability can sit far from the mean, built from nothing but the mean and the
variance, and pushed through to a weak law of large numbers.

- **[PROB-20 *Moment Generating and Characteristic Functions*]** — the concentration
  sibling. Where Chebyshev used only the first two moments, the MGF packages *all* of them,
  and applying Markov to $e^{tX}$ (instead of to $(X-\mu)^2$) upgrades Chebyshev's
  polynomial $1/k^2$ to the exponential **Chernoff** bound. Same engine — Markov on a
  cleverly chosen nonnegative function — sharper conclusion.
- **[PROB-21 *Modes of Convergence*]** — the immediate sequel. *Convergence in probability*,
  which @thm-wlln delivered for the sample mean, is defined and placed in its hierarchy
  there; Chebyshev is the standard tool for proving it.
- **[INFR-A2]** (Statistical Inference) — *owns* the **law of large numbers and the central
  limit theorem**. It consumes the inequality built here exactly as in @sec-wlln: Chebyshev
  supplies the distribution-free LLN, the CLT supplies the sharp rate. This is the clean
  ownership seam the track was designed around — Probability builds the inequality and the
  convergence vocabulary; Inference builds the limit theorems on top.
- **Backward links honored.** The variance-of-a-sum rule from [PROB-8] and the
  variance-scaling rule from [PROB-6] are what made $\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n) =
  \sigma^2/n$, the single fact the whole concentration payoff rests on.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary thread.** Bienaymé's 1853 memoir on least squares contained the variance bound;
  Chebyshev's 1867 paper *"Des valeurs moyennes"* used it to prove the law of large numbers
  without a normality assumption — the historical root of @sec-why-history. Markov's
  formalization of the nonnegative-variable bound followed in his probability lectures.
- **Chernoff and concentration inequalities.** Applying Markov to $e^{tX}$ and optimizing
  over $t$ gives the **Chernoff bound**; refining further for bounded variables gives
  **Hoeffding's** and **Bernstein's** inequalities, the exponential-tail backbone of
  modern statistical learning theory (generalization bounds, bandit algorithms). The whole
  field of *concentration of measure* is the rabbit hole that starts at the box in
  @sec-core.
- **One-sided Chebyshev (Cantelli).** A sharper, *asymmetric* version,
  $P(X - \mu \ge k\sigma) \le 1/(1+k^2)$, bounds a single tail and beats the two-sided
  $1/k^2$ when you only care about one direction — a clean exercise in re-running the
  Markov argument on $(X - \mu + t)^2$ and optimizing over $t$.
- **Open threads deferred.** *How much can you tighten the bound if you also know the third
  or fourth moment?* (higher-moment Markov: apply Markov to $|X-\mu|^p$). *What replaces
  Chebyshev when the variance is infinite but a fractional moment exists?* (the
  Marcinkiewicz–Zygmund laws and stable-law limits, downstream of heavy-tailed modeling).
  *How does the dependence between observations degrade the rate?* (mixing conditions and
  the ergodic-theorem version of the LLN).
